text.title: QA Prioritization Rubric Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the qa-prioritization-rubric methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with sourced rationale; the final rule is the skip-this-methodology gate referenced by the decision tree.

rule.statement: Severity ∈ {S0, S1, S2, S3}. No S4, no 'urgent', no 'minor'.
rule.rationale: Open severity vocabulary produces parallel scales; closed enum forces consistency.

rule.statement: S0=all users data-loss/security; S1=majority of users blocked; S2=minority blocked OR workaround exists; S3=cosmetic / edge.
rule.rationale: Without blast-radius anchors triage becomes opinion.

rule.statement: If a bug straddles two tiers by blast-radius, frequency breaks the tie (≥10% sessions = upgrade one tier).
rule.rationale: Single-incident bugs can be S2 even with low blast-radius if they recur constantly.

rule.statement: Existence of a documented workaround downgrades one tier (S1→S2, S2→S3). Undocumented workaround does not count.
rule.rationale: Documented workarounds reduce real user impact; undocumented ones don't help anyone.

rule.statement: S0=1h first response, 24h resolution; S1=24h first/72h resolution; S2=72h/1 sprint; S3=triage on next planning.
rule.rationale: SLAs make the rubric operational; rubric without SLAs is decorative.

rule.statement: Every triage decision cites the rule that anchored the tier (blast-radius, frequency, workaround).
rule.rationale: Uncited triages cannot be audited; cited ones can be challenged with evidence.

rule.statement: If solo project OR org rubric exists OR backlog<30, skip this methodology.
rule.rationale: Rubric overhead is wasted in those contexts.
